briefing.narrative:
The Head Monk sits cross-legged in the Grand Repository, surrounded by scrolls and three branching diagrams drawn in ink.

"Three feature branches," he says. "Each a mix of brilliance and catastrophe. The 'auth' branch has a good login system but also a commit that drops the database. The 'ui' branch has beautiful CSS but also a commit that replaces all text with wingdings. The 'api' branch... well, it's actually fine, just merge it."

He unfurls a final scroll. "Cherry-pick the good, revert the bad, stash if you must, and when the dust settles — tag the result as v3.0. This is your trial. The cat will judge your work."

The monastery cat puts on reading glasses. It is ready to judge.
briefing.concept: Real-world git work requires combining multiple skills: cherry-picking specific commits, reverting bad ones, managing stashes, and tagging releases. This level tests your ability to triage a messy repository and produce a clean, tagged release.
objectives: Cherry-pick the good commit from 'auth' (the login system, NOT the database drop)
Cherry-pick the good commit from 'ui' (the CSS, NOT the wingdings)
Tag the final result as 'v3.0' (annotated tag with a message)

args.content:
GET /monks - list all monks
POST /meditation - start session
DELETE /attachments - achieve enlightenment

hints.text: The Head Monk offers a clue: 'Not all commits on a branch are worth taking. Examine each branch with git log. Cherry-pick only the good. Tag when you are done.'
